citywideContext: NYC median 1BR crossed $4,000 for the first time in 2026; Manhattan median 1BR is roughly $4,400–4,700 and climbing, and inventory is at a ~4-year low — move fast on good listings.

east-village.note: Walkups dominate at $4K; large-building averages run ~$5.6K (Zumper).

lower-east-side.note: Tenement walkups list in the high-$3Ks; new towers skew the average.
lower-east-side.whatFourKGets: A tenement walkup in the high-$3Ks — the $5.4K average is skewed by new towers.

williamsburg.note: Big buildings average ~$5,286; the +12% YoY figure applies to large buildings (RentCafe, Apr 2026).
williamsburg.whatFourKGets: Under the $4,850 median — older or smaller units, not the big new buildings.

east-williamsburg.whatFourKGets: Just under the $4,350 median, but rents are rising fast at +18% a year.

greenpoint.whatFourKGets: Under the $4,675 median — smaller 1BRs, though rents have held flat.

bushwick.whatFourKGets: $4K rents above the median here — pick of the litter.

astoria.whatFourKGets: $4K is well above the $3,400 median — top-of-market space and choice.

long-island-city.note: Luxury-heavy inventory; verify via RentCast at build time.
long-island-city.whatFourKGets: The bottom of a luxury-heavy market — most inventory is new towers at $4.1K and up.

hells-kitchen.whatFourKGets: Under the $4,900 average — you trade square footage for the walk to work.

yorkville.note: UES median $4,725 all unit types; value pocket of Manhattan.
yorkville.whatFourKGets: A real shot at a 1BR — the range starts right at $4,000 in this Manhattan value pocket.

harlem.note: Essentially flat YoY (+0.35%) — one of the few real Manhattan value pockets, with a median near $3K.
harlem.whatFourKGets: $4K is a full $1K above the $2,998 median — top-of-market space and choice.

ridgewood.note: Down ~5% YoY; HotPads/Zillow shows a ~$2,658 1BR average. Thin listing volume — mostly small-landlord walkup and railroad stock.
ridgewood.whatFourKGets: $4K is far above the $2,838 median — pick of the market.

crown-heights.note: +10.3% YoY — rising fast; median of all active listings is $3,795.
crown-heights.whatFourKGets: Comfortably above the $3,650 median today, but +10% a year is closing that gap.

prospect-heights.note: Small neighborhood, thin inventory, figures swing: RentHop's median ($3,500, Jan) and RentCafe's large-building average ($4,819, Apr) are far apart; ApartmentAdvisor lands at ~$4,185.
prospect-heights.whatFourKGets: Workable in the older walkup stock; the new buildings average closer to $4.8K.

fort-greene.note: Slightly down YoY; RentCafe's large-building average is $4,740. The Clinton Hill side of this area medians $4,000 (RentHop, May 2026).
fort-greene.whatFourKGets: Right at the edge — $4K matches the Clinton Hill median but sits under Fort Greene proper's $4,261.

bed-stuy.note: +4.4% YoY; mostly brownstone and small-building stock rather than new towers.
bed-stuy.whatFourKGets: $4K is $800 above the median — a real brownstone floor-through, not a chopped-up share.

chelsea.note: One of Manhattan's priciest 1BR markets; RentCafe's average runs even higher (~$6,157).
chelsea.whatFourKGets: $4K is two-thirds of the $5,995 median — a small, dated walkup unit if anything.

west-village.note: Prewar walkups dominate — you pay for the address, not the square footage.
west-village.whatFourKGets: Long odds — $4K is $1.5K under the median; tiny prewar walkups only.
